redFlags.evidence: Looted from tumuli in the Uşak region of western Turkey in the 1960s.
The Met held the pieces in storage and obscured their origin for years.

repatriation.note: Returned by the Met to Turkey in 1993 after litigation.

identifyingRegisters.name: INTERPOL Stolen Works of Art
identifyingRegisters.requires: a theft reported to and recorded by a member-country police force

identifyingRegisters.name: INTERPOL ID-Art
identifyingRegisters.requires: the same police report as the Stolen Works of Art database

weakRegisters.name: ICOM Red Lists
weakRegisters.why: Lists object categories at risk, never individual objects — a due-diligence trigger, not an identification.

weakRegisters.name: Wikidata
weakRegisters.why: Coverage follows scholarly and press attention rather than rule, so its silence carries little information.

blindRegisters.name: FBI National Stolen Art File
blindRegisters.why: Holds market-theft losses; requires a law-enforcement submission and a value threshold.

blindRegisters.name: Carabinieri TPC
blindRegisters.why: Geographically out of scope for turkey — requires an Italian nexus — the archive is national.

blindRegisters.name: Lost Art Database
blindRegisters.why: Holds nazi-era losses; requires a loss connected to Nazi persecution, 1933–45.

blindRegisters.name: Getty Provenance Index
blindRegisters.why: Holds market, market-theft, nazi-era losses; requires passage through the European or American art market.

blindRegisters.name: Art Loss Register
blindRegisters.why: Holds market-theft, nazi-era losses; requires a registered loss, usually by an owner or insurer.

coverage.note: Only 2 of 9 registers could systematically name this object. A quiet result is weak evidence, not reassurance.
coverage.comparability: This score is comparable only to other partially covered objects. Comparison against a well-covered object will overstate how much was actually checked.
